# The Captain's Testimony

*A monologue. Captain Morrison stands at the bow of a weathered vessel, addressing a small gathering on deck. His voice carries the authority of four decades at sea.*

Not once.

Now, you listen to a man who's made his living reading water. Water finds its level. That's not theory, that's not ideology — that's the first thing any honest tradesman learns. A mason knows it. A carpenter knows it. Any fool with a garden hose knows it. You pour water into a basin, and it settles flat. You tip it, it runs. It does not — it *cannot* — rest in a curve. Water has no loyalty to any sphere. It obeys only its own nature, and its nature is to lie flat and still.

So tell me this. Tell me how an ocean — three thousand miles of it between here and England — how that ocean sits in a *bulge*. A bulge! They'd have you believe the Atlantic humps up in the middle like the back of some enormous beast, and that you and I and every ship that ever sailed are clinging sideways to the flanks of it. I've been in the middle of that ocean, friend. I've stood at the wheel at three in the morning with nothing but black water to every compass point. And I'll tell you what that water did. It lay down. Flat. Horizon to horizon. Flat as a millpond stretched to the edge of sight.

Now they'll come at you with their numbers. Eight inches per mile squared — that's the figure. Eight inches of drop for every mile, multiplied by the square of the distance. By their own arithmetic, a ship twenty miles out should be over *two hundred and sixty feet* below the curve. Two hundred and sixty feet! A ten-story building's worth of water between you and her hull.

And yet.

Hand me the glass on a clear day off the Carolina coast, and I'll show you vessels thirty, forty miles out — hull, waterline, and all — riding high and whole on the line of the sea. I've watched lighthouses from distances that, by their math, should have hidden them beneath miles of ocean. The light of the Isle of Wight from Port Said — I've read the old logs, I've seen the observations. Beams of light traveling flat across impossible distances, as if — and here's the heresy, friend — *as if the water they traveled over were flat.*

Because it is.

I've sailed the long reaches where a compass needle points true for a thousand miles and the sextant tells the same story day after day. I've taken soundings in channels that engineers surveyed a century ago with spirit levels — *spirit levels*, mind you — across canals running a hundred miles without one hair of curvature accounted for. The Suez. The old Bedford. Men laid those waterways with the assumption the water would behave, and the water *did* behave, because water does not bend itself around an imaginary ball to please a professor in a warm office.

You hear me? Those men built for a flat world and the flat world answered.

Now a young sailor asked me once — good lad, educated, full of the things they teach now — he asked me, "Captain, what about the ships that go down over the horizon? What about the mast disappearing last?" And I told him what any honest seaman will tell you if he's not afraid for his pension: that's perspective, son. That's your eye and the haze of the air playing the oldest trick in the book. Bring the glass up and you'll pull her right back into view — hull and all — a ship that your naked eye had "sunk behind the curve." A curve you can erase with a piece of ground glass is no curve at all. It's a trick of seeing, not a fact of the sea.

I have run before storms that the weather men said could not exist where they existed. I have watched the sun sink not *under* the sea but *away* across it — shrinking, receding, the way a lantern recedes down a long straight corridor. Any man who's watched a true sunset over open water with honest eyes has felt it, even if he couldn't say it. The sun doesn't fall behind the world. It travels out, away, across the plane, until distance and haze take it from us.

And the stars. Don't get me started on the stars. Polaris standing fixed above the northern ice while the rest wheel around her — fixed, mark you, fixed for every sailor for four thousand years — as if she hangs from a nail hammered into the center of everything. Because she does. Because we live on a plane, and she is our north.

I'll tell you what I believe, and I'll not soften it for polite company.

I believe the sea is a great flat water, bounded as all waters are bounded, and that we sail upon its face. I believe the men who first charted her knew it, and the men who sail her honestly now feel it in their bones whether they've the courage to say so or not. I believe the ball-earth is a beautiful piece of mathematics that has nothing to do with the wet, level, horizon-true world I have spent my life upon.

You want proof? Fill a glass. Set it on the table. Look at it.

That's the ocean, friend. That's every ocean I've ever crossed.

Flat.

And any man who tells you different has spent more time with a textbook than with the sea.

---

# In Defense of What My Own Eyes Can See
### An Essay by Marcus Hollander

For thirty-seven years I accepted what I was told. I sat in classrooms and nodded along with diagrams of a spinning ball hurtling through a vacuum. I memorized the numbers, recited the names of the planets, and never once asked the question that now seems so obvious to me: *Have you ever, in your entire life, seen water curve?*

I haven't. Neither have you. And that simple fact is the thread that, when pulled, unravels the whole tapestry.

Water finds its level. This is not a theory. It is not a hypothesis proposed in some university basement. It is a principle so reliable that we stake our homes and cities upon it. Every carpenter who has ever hung a shelf, every mason who has ever laid a foundation, every engineer who has ever dug a canal operates on the unshakeable knowledge that still water lies flat. The spirit level in my toolbox has never once read "curved." It reads level, because level is what water *is*.

Consider what the globe model demands of us. We are told that Earth's oceans—vast, restless, and obedient only to the physical laws that govern all fluids—somehow bend themselves around a ball. We are told that the surface of the Pacific, stretching thousands of miles, curves downward in every direction from any point you care to stand upon. Yet no sailor has ever sailed downhill. No captain has ever had to aim his vessel at an angle to compensate for the dip of the sea ahead. The horizon rises to meet the eye, flat and true, whether you stand at sea level or ascend a mountain.

The Bedford Level experiment, conducted by Samuel Rowbotham in the nineteenth century along a six-mile stretch of still canal water, demonstrated what any honest observer can verify: over miles of uninterrupted water, there is no curvature. A boat with a flag on its mast remained visible through a telescope at distances where, by the globe's own mathematics, it should have been hidden behind thousands of feet of bulging ocean. The math of the sphere predicts one thing. The water shows another. I know which I trust.

"But ships disappear hull-first over the horizon!" I hear the objection before it is spoken. Do they? Or do they simply recede beyond the limits of the human eye's resolution, only to be pulled back into view by a pair of good binoculars? I have tested this myself on the shores of Lake Michigan. What vanished to the naked eye returned, whole and intact, through the lens. Nothing had gone "behind" anything. It had simply grown small.

And lighthouses! The Statue of Liberty! The Chicago skyline, visible from across the lake on clear days at distances where, according to the curvature calculations, it should be buried beneath a wall of water taller than itself. The globe model does not merely fail these tests—it is humiliated by them. And yet we are asked to trust the model over the evidence of our senses, to trust the calculation over the thing calculated.

The response is always the same: *refraction*. Whenever water refuses to curve, whenever a distant city appears where it should be hidden, the defenders of the globe summon refraction like a magic word. But refraction is not a blanket excuse; it is a specific optical phenomenon with measurable parameters. It cannot be invoked whenever convenient and ignored whenever inconvenient. When the ball requires rescue, refraction appears. When it does not, refraction is silent. This is not science. This is theology with equations.

Return, always, to the water. Water is the great honest witness. It cannot lie, cannot be bribed, cannot be indoctrinated. Pour it into any vessel and it settles flat. Stretch it across any distance and it remains flat. Dam it, channel it, freeze it, and thaw it—it obeys one law, and that law is level. The oceans that cover most of this world are made of the same substance as the glass of water on my desk. What is true of one is true of the other.

I do not ask you to believe me. I ask you to believe the water. Fill your sink. Watch it settle. Understand that what you are seeing is the truth of the surface you stand upon, scaled down to fit your kitchen. The plane on which we live is precisely what it appears to be: a plane. Vast, yes. Strange in ways we are still discovering, certainly. But flat, as flat as the still surface of any pond, because it is made of the very same obedient water and earth that refuses, at every scale we can measure, to bend.

The burden of proof has never been mine. It belongs to those who insist that what I see is not what is there. Until they can show me water that curves—not in a diagram, not in an animation, but in the world—I will trust my eyes, my instruments, and the humble spirit level that has never once lied to me.

—*M. Hollander*

# On Level Ground: A Surveyor's Case for the Flat Earth

*An essay by Jonathan Gray*

---

I have spent thirty-one years with a theodolite in my hands. I have shot lines across lakes, laid out highways through river valleys, and set benchmarks along coasts from Maine to Louisiana. In all that time, across all those miles, I have never once had to adjust my calculations for the curvature of the Earth. Not once. And I intend, in the pages that follow, to tell you why that single professional fact should trouble every thinking person who has been told, since childhood, that we live on a spinning ball.

My thesis is simple: **the Earth is a flat, extended plane, and the behavior of water across large distances proves it beyond reasonable dispute.**

But if the Earth curves as we are told, then a river flowing east or west across a sufficient distance must at some point flow *uphill* relative to the center of the sphere, its waters pressed outward by the bulge. No such river has ever been surveyed to do so. Water runs downhill. Always. And "downhill," in every practical measurement ever taken, means toward a flat reference datum — what we in the profession call Mean Sea Level, a phrase that gives away the game. Level. Not curved. Level.

And here, before I go further, I must speak plainly about the question every honest man eventually asks: if the Earth is a plane, what holds the oceans in? Where do the waters end?

They end, as the old navigators' charts knew long before the modern cartographers redrew them, at a great rim of ice. What the globe-makers call "Antarctica" and present as a tidy disk at the bottom of their ball is, in truth, a vast circumferential wall — a ring of ice, hundreds of feet high in places, encircling the whole of the known lands and seas. It is the basin's edge. It is what keeps the Pacific from pouring out into whatever lies beyond. Any surveyor who has studied the published ice-shelf measurements, the sheer cliffs described by Ross and by later expeditions, can see the shape of the thing for himself if he is willing to set the globe aside for an afternoon and sketch it honestly.

Ask yourself then: why is this one region of the Earth — this single, enormous stretch of coastline — the most restricted territory on the planet? The Antarctic Treaty forbids independent exploration across the interior. Private vessels are turned back. Flight paths do not cross it; commercial aviation, which will route a plane over any patch of open ocean without a second thought, gives this region a wide and permanent berth. Tourists are escorted to a handful of approved coastal points and no further. Military patrols from several nations, otherwise rivals in every corner of the globe, cooperate here with perfect discipline to keep the curious away.

I have worked enough government contracts to know what restricted access means. When a fence goes up and the guards will not say what it guards, the fence itself is the evidence. Men do not spend treasure and manpower to keep the public away from nothing. The one feature of the Earth that would settle the question — that a man with a compass and a sledge could walk outward along and measure for himself — is precisely the feature no free man is permitted to approach. I leave it to the reader to decide whether that is a coincidence, or a confession.

The champions of the globe will point to ships "disappearing hull-first" over the horizon. I have watched a thousand ships on a thousand horizons. Bring a good telescope. The ship you thought had sailed over the edge of the world will return to full view, hull and all, pulled back from the vanishing point by nothing more than magnification. The horizon is a limit of human vision and atmospheric density, not a geometric wall. Perspective makes distant things appear to meet the ground; it does not prove the ground is round.

They will point to photographs from space. I would ask you how many of those photographs you have taken. I would ask you who paid for the cameras, who owns the agencies, who profits from the story. I do not ask this to be a cynic. I ask it because, as a surveyor, I am trained to trust the measurement I can repeat over the testimony I must take on faith. A man who has never leveled a transit has no business lecturing a man who has, about what lies at the end of his own sight line.

They will point to eclipses, to time zones, to circumnavigation. Each of these has an explanation consistent with a flat plane and a local, circuiting sun — explanations older than the globe model itself, worked out by navigators and almanac-makers long before any satellite was ever claimed to orbit. Time zones follow the sun's path across the plane. Circumnavigation traces a circle around the central point, not a loop around a ball. An airplane flying "east" long enough returns to its origin for the same reason a horse walking the rim of a corral returns to the gate.

So let the three great witnesses be heard together, for they speak with one voice.

The water speaks first, and says: I am flat. Across every canal, every lake, every ocean ever measured by an honest spirit level, I have never curved, and I will not begin curving now because a theorist requires it of me.

The ice speaks second, and says: I am the rim. I stand where the waters end. I am the reason the seas do not spill. And I am kept from you, behind treaties and patrols and quiet agreements among governments that agree on little else, because what I am would make a liar of every globe in every schoolroom.

The falling stone speaks last, and says: I need no invisible tether. I am denser than the air, and I seek my level as the balloon seeks his above me. The world is sorted by weight, not by magic, and the sorting happens just as readily on a plane as on any ball — more readily, in truth, because it requires no unseen force pulling toward no demonstrated center.

Water, ice, and stone. Level, wall, and weight. Three honest witnesses, none of them in the pay of any agency, none of them requiring a telescope pointed at the heavens or a grant application signed in triplicate. They have been telling the same story since men first thought to ask the question, and they tell it still, to any surveyor willing to set down his textbook and pick up his instruments.

I will trust my instruments. I will trust the water. I will trust the plumb bob and the balloon. I will trust the straight, patient line of sight that has fed my family for three decades.

The Earth beneath our feet is what it appears to be, what it measures to be, and what it has always, in every practical test, proven itself to be: a vast, level plane, rimmed by ice and governed by density, guarded from honest inquiry by those who profit from the lie. The burden of proof lies not with those of us who report what we see, but with those who insist we are seeing wrong.

I remain, as ever, on the level.

---

# On the Level: What Forty Years of Surveying Has Taught Me About the Shape of the World

*An essay by David Ward, Licensed Land Surveyor*

---

I have spent forty-three years with a theodolite in my hands. I have surveyed property lines across three counties, laid out the foundations of buildings that still stand, and verified elevations for roadworks, canals, and reservoirs. In all that time, across all those measurements, I have never once been required to account for the curvature of the Earth. Not once. And I am going to tell you why.

The men who trained me — old-timers who'd been running transits since before the war — had a saying: *water finds its level.* Not its curve. Not its arc. Its **level**. And any surveyor worth his salt knows this in his bones, because the moment you doubt it, your work falls apart. The spirit level in your instrument, the plumb bob hanging from your string, the still surface of a settling pond — these are the references by which every structure in the modern world has been built. If they lied to us, nothing would stand.

Consider what we are asked to believe. We are told that the oceans — vast, restless, unimaginable in scale — are in truth curved around a sphere, bulging in the middle, falling away at the edges. We are told that across every mile of water, the surface drops some eight inches from true level, and that this drop compounds with the square of the distance. Six miles out, the math demands twenty-four feet of curvature. Across the English Channel, hundreds of feet. Across the Atlantic, miles.

And yet.

I invite any honest man to visit the Bedford Level, as Rowbotham did, and repeat his experiment. A straight canal, six miles long, with markers of known height. Sight along it with a good glass. The far marker should, according to the spheroid theory, be buried two dozen feet beneath your line of sight. It is not. It sits where level says it should sit. I have done versions of this test myself on reservoirs in my own work, because a surveyor who does not verify his references is no surveyor at all. The water is flat. It is flat in the morning and flat in the evening. It is flat at one mile and flat at ten. I have seen the lights of distant shores from low vantages where they have no business being visible if the globe were real — lighthouses that should be swallowed by hundreds of feet of ocean bulge, yet there they burn, clear as a candle on a table.

The defenders of the spheroid will tell you about refraction. Always refraction. Whenever an observation inconveniences the theory, the atmosphere is summoned to bend the light by exactly the amount required to rescue the model. I find this suspicious in the extreme. In forty-three years I have never been permitted to invoke refraction to fudge a property line. My clients would laugh me out of the room. Yet an entire cosmology leans on it.

Then there is the matter of engineering. I have read the specifications for long canals, for transcontinental railways, for suspension bridges whose towers stand thousands of feet apart. Nowhere in the working drawings I have handled have I ever seen an allowance for the curvature of the Earth built into the design. Engineers build for level. Level is what holds. If the planet truly curved beneath these works, the works would tear themselves apart within a season. They do not. The Suez Canal was cut without compensating for a supposed bulge of water at its midpoint. It runs. It carries ships. The water sits flat along its entire length.

I am aware of what I am accused of when I say these things. I have been called a crank. I have been told, politely and otherwise, that satellites and photographs and the testimony of astronauts settle the question. But I am a surveyor, and I trust what I can measure with my own hands. Photographs can be made to show anything. Testimony can be purchased or pressured. The instrument in my hands cannot lie — and when I place it on the shore of a great lake and sight across to the far side, the far side does not dip. It stands where level predicts. The water between is a plane.

A plane, gentlemen. Not a curve.

I do not ask the reader to take my word. I ask only that you go and look. Find a long, still body of water. Bring a decent glass. Sight a distant object near the waterline — a boat, a pier, a building on the far shore. Note what your own eyes tell you, before the textbooks get a chance to explain it away. The water will be flat. It has always been flat. It will always be flat. And a world whose waters are flat is a world that is flat, for water is the great leveler and will not lie to suit a theory.

I have trusted the spirit bubble for forty-three years. It has never once failed me. I will trust it until the day they bury me — on ground that is, I can assure you, perfectly level.

---

*David Ward has worked as a licensed surveyor in the Midlands since 1981.*

# The Water Never Lies
### An Essay by John Hartwell, Licensed Surveyor

*[A work of fiction. The following is written in the voice of a character and does not reflect scientific reality.]*

---

I have spent forty-one years with a transit and a level. I have laid out highways through three states, staked foundations for buildings you have almost certainly walked into, and run property lines across ranches larger than some European countries. In all that time, through all those miles, I have learned one thing with the certainty of a man who has bet his professional reputation on it thousands of times over: water finds its level, and its level is flat.

Let me explain what that means, because the word "level" has been quietly stolen from us.

When I set up my instrument and shoot a line across a reservoir, I am not measuring a curve. I am measuring a plane. If the globe model were correct, the surface of any sufficiently long body of still water would bulge upward in the middle and fall away at the edges. A lake six miles across would hump up roughly six feet in the center. The Bedford Level experiment, conducted on a straight six-mile stretch of the Old Bedford River in England, found no such bulge. None. The surface was flat. I have replicated the principle myself, at smaller scales, more times than I can count, and the water has never once betrayed a curve.

Consider what engineers actually do when they build. Canals are cut for hundreds of miles without any accounting for curvature. The Suez Canal, one hundred miles of continuous water, was engineered as a level cut from sea to sea. No locks. No compensating arcs. The Grand Canal of China, over a thousand miles. If the Earth curved as claimed, these projects would be impossible as designed—yet there they sit, carrying ships across supposedly curving seas on surfaces that are demonstrably flat.

The defenders of the globe will tell you that engineers "account for curvature." I have read the textbooks. I have read the field manuals. I have trained young surveyors for most of my career. And I can tell you plainly: in practice, over the distances that matter to real construction, the correction is either ignored entirely or applied as a ritual that never actually changes the outcome. The bridges stand. The pipelines flow. The railways run true. They run true because the ground beneath them is not what we have been told it is.

Water is the honest witness here, and water cannot be intimidated by consensus. Pour it into any vessel and it settles into a plane perpendicular to the pull of gravity. Now scale that vessel up. Scale it up to a pond, a lake, a sea, an ocean. At what point, exactly, does water abandon its nature and begin to wrap itself around a ball? At what magical threshold does a liquid that has spent every moment of its existence seeking flatness suddenly agree to curve? No one can tell you, because no one has ever observed it. The curve is asserted. The flatness is measured.

I have stood on the shore of Lake Pontchartrain and watched, through a good telescope, the transmission towers on the far side—towers that, by the globe calculation, should have their lower two hundred feet hidden below the bulge of the water. They are not hidden. I have seen the Chicago skyline from across Lake Michigan, sixty miles away, when the globe model insists it should be buried beneath nearly two thousand feet of curvature. The buildings stand there in full, from foundation to spire, exactly as a flat plane would present them.

They will tell you it is "refraction." Refraction has become the great catch-all, the universal solvent used to dissolve any observation that threatens the model. But refraction is a specific optical phenomenon with specific conditions, not a magic wand to be waved whenever water refuses to curve. A surveyor learns early to account for refraction in precise work; it is a small, measurable correction, not the wholesale reconstruction of reality that the globe defenders require it to be.

Here is what I know, and what I would stake my license on: if you take an instrument you trust, set it carefully, and shoot a level line across any still water of any length you please, that line will run along the surface of the water. Not above it in the middle. Not below it at the edges. Along it. The water and the line will agree, because both are obeying the same simple truth—that the world beneath our feet is a plane, and that every honest measurement ever taken on it has said so.

The globe is a model. The flat water is a fact. When a model contradicts a fact, it is not the fact that must yield.

I have spent my life reading the land through instruments that do not lie, in a profession where a mistake of an inch can cost a man his livelihood. I did not come to this view through rebellion or romance. I came to it the same way I came to every other conclusion in my career: by looking carefully, measuring honestly, and trusting what the water told me.

The water has been telling us, all along, that it is flat.

We should start listening.

### An Essay by John Hartwell, Licensed Surveyor

The flatness is measured.

I say: no. I say the oceans lie flat because water lies flat. I say that what is true in a teacup is true in a sea, for the laws of nature do not take holidays at convenient scales. If you tell me water curves over eight thousand miles, show me the curve in eight feet. Show me the curve in eighty. You cannot. No engineer surveying a canal has ever had to account for it. The Bedford Level, that long straight ribbon of English water, was measured by Rowbotham and found flat as a tabletop, and for this honesty he was mocked by men who had never waded into it.

They invoke it the way priests once invoked demons—to explain away whatever the plain senses refuse to accept. I trust my eyes. I trust the carpenter's level and the mason's plumb line and the still surface of a pond at dawn. These have never lied to me. The globe has.

I would rather be a lonely witness to what is true than a celebrated citizen of what is false. The Earth is a plane. The waters rest upon it as waters rest in any vessel. The sun and moon move above it as lamps move above a stage. # On the Stillness Beneath Our Feet
### by Arthur Vance

I did not come to this conviction lightly. For most of my life I accepted, as you likely do now, the picture handed to me in childhood: a blue marble spinning through the void, tilted just so, hurtling around a star at speeds I could not fathom. Tilt the basin, and the water rushes to find its level again — flat, always flat, answering to some deep law written into the nature of the thing itself. This is not controversial. Say the words slowly. *Water finds its level.*

Now I ask you to consider the oceans.

We are told — and asked to believe with the solemnity once reserved for scripture — that the waters of the Earth curve. That the Pacific, that vast and trembling expanse, bulges outward in a dome thousands of miles across, conforming itself to the shape of a ball. I ask you plainly: have you ever, in any experiment of your own, seen water form a convex surface and hold it? Have you seen it arch itself against a wall of nothing? Of course you have not. No one has. The behavior of water is among the most consistent phenomena known to man, and its behavior is to lie flat.

Consider the Bedford Level. A six-mile stretch of still canal in England, straight as a ruler. In the nineteenth century, a man named Rowbotham waded out with a telescope and observed boats along its length. On a ball of the dimensions we are given, the far end of that canal should drop some twenty-four feet below the near end — a curvature easily detected, easily measured. He found no such curvature. The water lay flat, as water does. The boats did not sink behind a hill of sea. They grew small with distance, as all things do, and then a telescope brought them back again — which is impossible, absolutely impossible, if they had sailed over the shoulder of a globe.

Consider the lighthouses. The Statue of Liberty, the skylines of distant cities photographed across bodies of water at ranges where, by the accepted mathematics, they ought to be submerged beneath hundreds of feet of hidden ocean. They are not submerged. They stand visible, stubborn, rebuking the model that says they should not be there.

And consider, finally, what we are asked to swallow concerning motion. We are told this Earth spins upon an axis at roughly a thousand miles an hour. That it simultaneously hurtles around the sun at sixty-seven thousand miles an hour. That the sun itself drags the whole arrangement through the galaxy at speeds that make the mind reel. A bullet fired straight up returns near to the shooter, when by rights the Earth should have spun a quarter mile out from under him while it traveled.

The reply is always the same: atmosphere. The atmosphere carries us along. The atmosphere matches the spin exactly. A convenient atmosphere, this — one that clings tight enough to drag every molecule of smoke and every feather in perfect lockstep with a rotating stone, and yet somehow yields to the lightest breeze and the softest breath. It is an answer invented to rescue a theory, and an answer invented to rescue a theory is not an answer at all. It is a confession.

I know what is said of men like me. I know the word reached for — I will not dignify it by writing it. I have been a reasonable man all my life. I paid my taxes. I raised my children. I believed what I was told until the day I stopped believing it, and the day I stopped was the day I set a spirit level on the window of an airplane at cruising altitude and watched the bubble hold perfectly center for six hours across an ocean that, by all accounts, should have required the nose of that craft to dip continuously to follow its curve.

The Earth on which we stand is still. It is vast beyond our reckoning. And it is, as every drop of water upon it testifies, flat.

Every engineer who has ever laid a foundation, every mason who has ever set a stone, every surveyor who has ever stretched a line has relied upon it. Water finds its level. Say the words slowly. *Water finds its level.*

Now I ask you to consider the oceans.

We are told — and asked to believe with the solemnity once reserved for scripture — that the waters of the Earth curve. That the Pacific, that vast and trembling expanse, bulges outward in a dome thousands of miles across, conforming itself to the shape of a ball. That a ship sailing from San Francisco to Yokohama climbs and descends invisible hills of saltwater, bound there by a force we cannot see, cannot feel, and cannot, in any laboratory on this Earth, replicate in a bucket.

I ask you plainly: have you ever, in any experiment of your own, seen water form a convex surface and hold it? Have you seen it arch itself against a wall of nothing? Of course you have not. No one has. The behavior of water is among the most consistent phenomena known to man, and its behavior is to lie flat.

In the nineteenth century, a man named Rowbotham waded out with a telescope and observed boats along its length. On a ball of the dimensions we are given, the far end of that canal should drop some twenty-four feet below the near end — a curvature easily detected, easily measured. He found no such curvature.
